result_equations.equation_title: Fundamental Poisson Bracket of New Coordinates and Momenta
Hamiltonian Transformation for a Type-2 Generating Function

explanation: Canonical transformations are differentiable, invertible changes of phase-space coordinates `(q,p) -> (Q,P)` that preserve the canonical form of Hamilton's equations. Equivalently, they preserve the fundamental Poisson brackets and their Jacobian is symplectic. Locally they can be generated by functions such as `F_2(q,P,t)`, which also determines the transformed Hamiltonian through `K = H + (del F_2)/(del t)`.

definitions.definition: New generalized coordinate associated with the `i`-th degree of freedom.
Poisson bracket with respect to the old canonical variables `(q_i,p_i)`: `PB(u,v) = sum_(k=1)^n ((del u)/(del q_k)*(del v)/(del p_k) - (del u)/(del p_k)*(del v)/(del q_k))`.
Kronecker delta, equal to 1 when `i = j` and 0 otherwise.
Hamiltonian expressed in the new canonical variables `(Q,P)` after the transformation.
Hamiltonian expressed in the old canonical variables `(q,p)`.
Type-2 generating function `F_2(q,P,t)` used to construct a canonical transformation.
Jacobian matrix `M = (del epsilon)/(del eta)` of the transformation on a fixed-time phase-space slice, where `eta = (q_1, ..., q_n, p_1, ..., p_n)^T` and `epsilon = (Q_1, ..., Q_n, P_1, ..., P_n)^T`.
Old generalized coordinate associated with the `i`-th degree of freedom.
Number of degrees of freedom of the mechanical system.

derivation.description: Start from Hamilton's equations in the old canonical variables `(q_i,p_i)`, using the dependency `hamiltons_equations`.
A transformation `(q,p) -> (Q,P)` is canonical if there exists a Hamiltonian `K(Q,P,t)` such that Hamilton's equations keep the same form in the new variables.

For the two canonical descriptions to produce the same stationary trajectories, their Poincare-Cartan integrands may differ only by a total time derivative of a type-1 generating function `F_1(q,Q,t)`.
derivation.equation: sum_(i=1)^n p_i*(dq_i)/(dt) - H = sum_(i=1)^n P_i*(dQ_i)/(dt) - K + (dF_1)/(dt)

derivation.description: Expand the total derivative of `F_1(q,Q,t)` by the chain rule.
derivation.equation: (dF_1)/(dt) = sum_(i=1)^n (del F_1)/(del q_i)*(dq_i)/(dt) + sum_(i=1)^n (del F_1)/(del Q_i)*(dQ_i)/(dt) + (del F_1)/(del t)

derivation.description: Substitute step 4 into step 3 and collect the independent coefficients of `(dq_i)/(dt)` and `(dQ_i)/(dt)`.
derivation.equation: sum_(i=1)^n (p_i - (del F_1)/(del q_i))*(dq_i)/(dt) + sum_(i=1)^n (-P_i - (del F_1)/(del Q_i))*(dQ_i)/(dt) + K - H - (del F_1)/(del t) = 0

derivation.description: Because the velocities `(dq_i)/(dt)` and `(dQ_i)/(dt)` are independent, each coefficient in step 5 must vanish separately. This gives the type-1 generating-function relations.
derivation.equation: p_i = (del F_1)/(del q_i), quad P_i = - (del F_1)/(del Q_i), quad K = H + (del F_1)/(del t)

derivation.description: Define the type-2 generating function `F_2(q,P,t) = F_1(q,Q,t) + sum_(i=1)^n Q_i P_i`. Using step 6, its differential becomes a one-form involving `dq_i`, `dP_i`, and `dt` only.
derivation.equation: dF_2 = dF_1 + sum_(i=1)^n Q_i dP_i + sum_(i=1)^n P_i dQ_i = sum_(i=1)^n p_i dq_i + sum_(i=1)^n Q_i dP_i + (K - H) dt

derivation.description: Since `F_2` depends on `(q,P,t)`, its total differential can also be written in terms of its natural variables.
derivation.equation: dF_2 = sum_(i=1)^n (del F_2)/(del q_i) dq_i + sum_(i=1)^n (del F_2)/(del P_i) dP_i + (del F_2)/(del t) dt

derivation.description: Compare steps 7 and 8 coefficient by coefficient. This yields the type-2 generating-function relations, including the transformed Hamiltonian formula.
derivation.equation: p_i = (del F_2)/(del q_i), quad Q_i = (del F_2)/(del P_i), quad K = H + (del F_2)/(del t)

derivation.description: On each fixed-time phase-space slice, define `eta = (q_1, ..., q_n, p_1, ..., p_n)^T` and `epsilon = (Q_1, ..., Q_n, P_1, ..., P_n)^T`. Let `M = (del epsilon)/(del eta)` be the Jacobian of the transformation. Here `grad_eta` and `grad_epsilon` denote gradients with respect to `eta` and `epsilon`. For any scalar function `u`, the chain rule gives the gradient transformation.
Write the Poisson bracket in matrix form with respect to the old canonical variables, using the standard symplectic matrix `J`.

Substitute the chain-rule relation from step 10 into step 11. Requiring the Poisson bracket to have the same canonical form in the new variables for all functions `u` and `v` forces the Jacobian to satisfy the symplectic condition.
derivation.equation: PB_(eta)(u,v) = (grad_epsilon u)^T M J M^T grad_epsilon v = PB_(epsilon)(u,v) implies M J M^T = J

derivation.description: Apply step 12 to the coordinate functions `u = Q_i` and `v = P_j`. The `(i,n+j)` block of `J` equals `delta_(ij)`, so the mixed bracket has the canonical value.
Apply step 12 to `u = Q_i` and `v = Q_j`. The coordinate-coordinate block of `J` vanishes, so the bracket is zero.
Apply step 12 to `u = P_i` and `v = P_j`. The momentum-momentum block of `J` also vanishes, so the bracket is zero.

code: from sympy import Matrix, eye, zeros, diff, expand, simplify
# Steps 10-12 derive the symplectic condition from bracket invariance.
# Steps 13-15 verify the fundamental Poisson brackets.
# For one degree of freedom, the action-integrand relation is
# Expanding dF_1/dt and collecting the independent velocities qdot and Qdot
F1_q, F1_Q, F1_t = sp.symbols('F1_q F1_Q F1_t', real=True)
# Define F_2 = F_1 + Q*P. Using dF_1 = p*dq - P*dQ + (K-H)dt,
dq, dQ, dP, dt = sp.symbols('dq dQ dP dt', real=True)
F2_q, F2_P, F2_t = sp.symbols('F2_q F2_P F2_t', real=True)
# Steps 10-12: Matrix form of the Poisson bracket and symplectic condition
pb_rewritten = grad_u_eps.T * M * J * M.T * grad_v_eps
assert pb_rewritten.subs(M * J * M.T, J) == pb_eps
# Steps 13-15: Fundamental brackets and symplectic condition in 1 DOF
# For Q = a*q + b*p and P = c*q + d*p, compute the brackets exactly.
a, b, c, d = sp.symbols('a b c d', real=True)
return simplify(diff(u, q)*diff(v, p_old) - diff(u, p_old)*diff(v, q))
assert simplify(M1 * J1 * M1.T - (a*d - b*c) * J1) == zeros(2, 2)
assert simplify((M1 * J1 * M1.T).subs(a*d - b*c, 1) - J1) == zeros(2, 2)
# Additional 2-DOF examples: exchange and scaling transformations.
q1, q2, p1, p2, alpha = sp.symbols('q1 q2 p1 p2 alpha', real=True, nonzero=True)
assert simplify(M_exchange * J * M_exchange.T - J) == zeros(4, 4)
assert simplify(M_scale * J * M_scale.T - J) == zeros(4, 4)

historical_context.importance: Canonical transformations expose the symplectic geometry of classical mechanics and provide the natural language for action-angle variables, perturbation theory, and the Hamilton-Jacobi method.
key_insights: Hamiltonian dynamics is invariant under a distinguished class of phase-space coordinate changes.
Generating functions provide a constructive method for building canonical transformations.
The Jacobian of a canonical transformation is symplectic, so the Poisson-bracket structure is preserved.
Canonical transformations connect directly to the Hamilton-Jacobi equation and to modern symplectic geometry.

references.citation: Jacobi, C. G. J. (1837). Zur Theorie der Variations-Rechnung und der Differential-Gleichungen. Journal für die reine und angewandte Mathematik, 17, 68-82.
Goldstein, H., Poole, C., & Safko, J. (2002). Classical Mechanics (3rd ed.). Addison-Wesley.
Arnold, V. I. (1989). Mathematical Methods of Classical Mechanics (2nd ed.). Springer.
